title: MSc Thesis - Memory-Efficient KV Cache Compression for LLM Inference
outcome: Cutting the KV-cache GPU-memory bottleneck for long-context LLM inference
summary: Master's thesis at Universitaet Trier on memory-efficient LLM inference, compressing the runtime KV cache to ease the GPU-memory bottleneck at long context. Method and results are unpublished.
detail: At long context the KV cache becomes the dominant GPU-memory cost of large-language-model inference, which caps how long a context a model can serve on a given GPU. My thesis develops a way to compress this runtime cache substantially while preserving generation quality.
The work is ongoing and not yet published, so the specific method and results are kept private for now. I am happy to walk through the approach and findings on request.

title: RAG Resume API
outcome: Powers the live AI Q&A assistant embedded in this portfolio site
summary: A Retrieval-Augmented Generation API over my resume and documents: LlamaIndex + Gemini 2.5 Flash, FastAPI on Cloud Run, with rate limiting and a daily budget cap.
detail: A static portfolio site gave visitors no interactive way to ask about my background, projects or experience, so I built a production-safe Retrieval-Augmented Generation backend that indexes my personal documents and answers questions in real time.

It uses a LlamaIndex vector store with gemini-embedding-001 for embeddings and gemini-2.5-flash for generation, served via FastAPI, containerised with Docker and deployed on Google Cloud Run. The live endpoint adds per-IP rate limiting, a daily Gemini budget cap, an in-memory query cache and Google Sheets request logging, and powers the Q&A box on this site with scale-to-zero free-tier hosting.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Narrative Similarity (HeteroGNN + Text Fusion)
outcome: 72.5% pairwise narrative-similarity accuracy with an interpretable additive fusion
summary: Fuses a HeteroGNN over LLM-extracted narrative components with Gemini text embeddings to score narrative similarity, with an interpretable additive decomposition.
detail: Narrative similarity methods typically rely on fine-tuned transformer embeddings that produce opaque dense vectors with no structural interpretability. This project combines a graph channel over LLM-extracted narrative components with a neural text channel.

GPT-OSS 20B extracts narrative components into schema-validated JSON, a heterogeneous graph with four node types is built from them, and a HeteroGNN with SAGEConv and triplet loss is trained and then fused with Gemini text embeddings via normalised averaging with CORAL domain adaptation. It reaches 72.5% accuracy on pairwise narrative similarity, and a principal-angle analysis shows the graph and text channels occupy near-orthogonal subspaces (85.4 degrees), giving an exact additive decomposition of the fused cosine score.

title: FEAR - Asymmetric RLVR for LLM Calibration and Abstention
outcome: Finding O: under GRPO, asymmetric RLVR rewards collapse to one abstention-threshold knob
summary: Research testing whether punishment-dominant asymmetric RLVR rewards improve LLM calibration and abstention; built a full GRPO training and calibration pipeline on Qwen 2.5.
detail: Prior work suggested that asymmetric, abstention-aware reinforcement-learning rewards, a large penalty for a wrong answer and a small reward for a correct one, could make language models better calibrated and more willing to abstain when uncertain, but this had not been tested rigorously.

The project built a full GRPO training and calibration-evaluation pipeline on Qwen 2.5 1.5B and 3B, sweeping seven reward conditions across six math and reasoning benchmarks with from-scratch calibration metrics (ECE, Brier, AUROC, AUACC) and advantage-normalisation ablations, and trained and pushed more than 13 LoRA adapters to Hugging Face.

The answer was no: asymmetry only shifted how often the model abstained, not its calibration. The contribution, Finding O, is the mechanistic reason: under GRPO with within-group z-score normalisation the reward design collapses to a single abstention-threshold dial, which explains the field's convergence on simple positive-only rewards. The result was confirmed at 3B and stopped at that scale on compute limits.

title: Amazon Electronics Recommender System
outcome: NDCG@10 0.305 -> 0.334 (+9.5%) with a Two-Tower MLP, sub-100ms CPU serving
summary: End-to-end recommender on 390K Amazon Electronics interactions: Two-Tower MLP, FAISS retrieval + MLP re-rank, FastAPI on Azure with sub-100ms CPU serving.
detail: Building a scalable recommender on the Amazon Electronics dataset (41K users, 137K items and about 390K interactions) meant combining implicit feedback with product metadata and text while keeping inference cheap enough for CPU-only cloud deployment.

I implemented and compared collaborative filtering, hybrid SBERT and metadata models, and a Two-Tower MLP, fused SBERT title embeddings into the item tower, and built a two-stage FAISS retrieval and MLP re-rank pipeline, bundling the inference assets into a single checkpoint that cut cold start from 30-45s to 2-3s and deploying on Azure Container Apps via FastAPI. The Two-Tower MLP improved NDCG@10 from 0.305 to 0.334 (about 9.5% relative, validated with paired t-tests over 5 seeds) and lifted cold-start hit rate by roughly 50%, with sub-100ms warm inference on CPU. The live demo may cold-start from scale-to-zero, so the first request can take up to about 30s.

title: Reflective CoT Faithfulness Study (Think Twice)
outcome: RCoT cut bias susceptibility by ~7 points over standard CoT across 650 BBH cases
summary: A reproducible framework testing whether reflective chain-of-thought makes LLM reasoning more faithful, across 13 BIG-Bench Hard categories and two models.
detail: Earlier work showed that chain-of-thought rationales can be swayed by irrelevant prompt cues, but whether structured self-reflection reduces this systematic unfaithfulness was untested.

I co-built a reproducible evaluation framework running Gemini 2.5 Flash Lite and LLaMA 3.2 3B over 13 BIG-Bench Hard categories (650 instances), comparing direct, chain-of-thought and reflective chain-of-thought prompting under three bias-injection conditions in zero-shot and few-shot settings with structured JSON output enforcement. Reflective CoT reduced bias susceptibility by roughly 7 percentage points over standard CoT, but the models still produced plausible fabricated rationales that hid the true cause of biased predictions, showing that structured reflection alone is not enough for faithful reasoning.

title: Risk-Averse Optimization (CVaR across 3 Domains)
outcome: CVaR MPC cut constraint violations from 100% to 0% under severe model mismatch
summary: CVaR-based risk-averse optimisation across three domains: nonlinear programming, an SVM classifier, and a 21-day wine-fermentation MPC controller.
detail: Standard nominal optimisation ignores tail risk and produces fragile solutions when real-world parameters deviate from assumptions, a problem that recurs in static nonlinear programs, machine-learning classifiers and dynamic control alike.

This case study applied Conditional Value at Risk objectives via the Rockafellar-Uryasev form with sample-average approximation across three domains: a Wobbly Rosenbrock program in CasADi and IPOPT, an SVM on a cats-vs-dogs dataset in CVXPY and OSQP, and a wine-fermentation nonlinear ODE as a scenario-based receding-horizon controller over a 21-day horizon. Under severe temperature mismatch the CVaR controller cut constraint violations from 100% to 0%, and the CVaR-SVM improved mean accuracy from 54.33% to 56.33% while tightening the tail against both Gaussian and heavy-tailed perturbations.

title: ML Kaggle Competitions (Top-3 in Three Challenges)
outcome: Top-3 finish in all three university Kaggle challenges
summary: Top-3 in three university Kaggle challenges spanning classical ML (HistGBM), a from-scratch PyTorch CNN, and few-shot LLM classification with Gemma-3-12B.
detail: Three course-integrated Kaggle competitions called for competitive solutions under hard tooling constraints, each covering a distinct paradigm: tabular machine learning, image classification and LLM-based inference.

I built reproducible single-file solutions for each, engineering temporal and cost features for a HistGradientBoostingClassifier in the first, designing a VGG-style CNN with AdamW, cosine learning-rate scheduling and augmentation (no pretrained weights) in the second, and using few-shot retrieval prompting with a 4-bit quantised Gemma-3-12B in the third. The solutions placed top-3 in all three competitions, spanning classical machine learning, deep learning and practical LLM inference.

title: Transformer-Based Sentiment Analysis (YouTube Comments)
outcome: F1 = 0.81 on 10,000+ imbalanced YouTube comments, CI/CD to Docker Hub
summary: Fine-tuned BERT for YouTube comment sentiment, served via a Flask API, containerised with Docker, and shipped through GitHub Actions to Docker Hub.
detail: YouTube comment datasets are large, noisy and class-imbalanced, which makes accurate sentiment classification a practical NLP challenge with no readily deployable solution.

I fine-tuned a BERT model on more than 10,000 YouTube comments with PyTorch and Hugging Face, served it through a Flask REST API in a Docker container, and automated image builds and pushes to Docker Hub with GitHub Actions, alongside an interactive Gradio demo on Hugging Face Spaces. The model reached an F1 of 0.81 on the imbalanced dataset, with a CI/CD pipeline that turns every code change into an updated, published image for repeatable deployment.

title: Glaucoma Detection from Retinal Fundus Images
outcome: Published at ICCSP 2020 (IEEE): region-growing segmentation + SVM screening
summary: Automated glaucoma detection from retinal fundus images using optic-disc/cup segmentation and SVM classification. Undergraduate work, published at ICCSP 2020.
detail: Glaucoma is a leading cause of blindness, and manual screening from retinal fundus images is time-consuming and needs specialist expertise.

Leading a four-member team, I built a region-growing segmentation pipeline for optic-disc and cup extraction followed by SVM classification, implemented in MATLAB. The work was accepted and presented at the International Conference on Communication and Signal Processing (ICCSP) 2020 and published on IEEE Xplore, the team's first peer-reviewed publication.
